EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moved through the eastern U.S. bringing a plume of deep moisture over top of an arctic airmass which brough 1-2 feet of snow to southern New England Sunday through Tuesday morning. The biggest total was 27 inches in Gloucester, MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 12 (lowlands) to 20 (high terrain) of inches fell with some of the highest totals being 21 inches in Simsbury and 20 inches in Bloomfield. Some of the lower amounts were 11.3 inches in West Hartford and 12.5 inches in Bristol.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moved through the eastern U.S. bringing a plume of deep moisture over top of an arctic airmass which brough 1-2 feet of snow to southern New England Sunday through Tuesday morning. The biggest total was 27 inches in Gloucester, MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 14 to 19 inches of snow fell with the heaviest being 19.1 inches in Columbia and 10 inches in Tolland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure moved through the eastern U.S. bringing a plume of deep moisture over top of an arctic airmass which brough 1-2 feet of snow to southern New England Sunday through Tuesday morning. The biggest total was 27 inches in Gloucester, MA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 15 to 19 inches of snow fell with some of the highest amounts being 19.5 inches in West Thompson Lake and 16.8 inches in South Windham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along a strong cold front which triggered snowfall, with accumulating snow mainly confined to eastern portions of Southern Connecticut. Advisory level snow occurred primarily across portions of New London County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observers reported 3.2 inches in New London, 2.8 inches in Mystic, 3.5 inches in East Lyme. The public reported 3.0 inches at 2 SSE Ledyard Center. A trained spotter reported 3.0 inches at 1 SSW New London.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along a strong cold front which triggered snowfall, with accumulating snow mainly confined to eastern portions of Southern Connecticut. Advisory level snow occurred primarily across portions of New London County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observers reported 3.0 inches at Killingworth and 3.0 inches at 2.9 NNE Old Saybrook.  Broadcast media reported 2.5 inches a 2 S Killingworth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elongated low pressure which eventually intensified offshore along a cold front brought an low end advisory accumulating snowfall to Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An official NWS observation of 4.0 at Bridgeport Airport was reported.  Trained spotter reported 3.5 inches at 2 ENE New Canaan and 2.7 inches at 3 SSE Weston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elongated low pressure which eventually intensified offshore along a cold front brought an low end advisory accumulating snowfall to Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported 3.3 inches at 4 NNW New Fairfield and 3.5 inches at 3 WSW Shelton.  A cocorahs observer reported 2.5 inches at 1.2 E Sherman and 2.5 inches in Bethel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system emerged out of the Southern Plains and Texas and rode northeast through the Tennessee Valley and the Appalachians, before emerging just off the New Jersey coast. This produced a heavy snowfall across all of Southern Connecticut, with sleet, some of it heavy across a portion of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 15.7 inches at 2 NNW Bethel, 15.5 inches at 1 ESE Danbury, 15.0 inches at 4 SSE Sherman, 13.7 inches at 1 E Monroe, and 13.0 inches at 3 WSW Shelton. Snowfall amounts were fairly uniform across the zone despite the high accumulation totals with average amounts estimated to be just above a foot of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system emerged out of the Southern Plains and Texas and rode northeast through the Tennessee Valley and the Appalachians, before emerging just off the New Jersey coast. This produced a heavy snowfall across all of Southern Connecticut, with sleet, some of it heavy across a portion of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official NWS observation from Bridgeport Airport was 15.3 inches. Trained spotters reported 10.5 inches at 4 WNW Darien, 10.0 inches at 2 NNW Stamford, 12.6 inches at 2 ENE New Canaan, and 12.9 inches at 3 SSE Weston.  Cocorahs reported 11.7 inches in Norwalk, 10.0 inches in Stratford, and 11.0 inches in Darien. Average snowfall totals across the zone were estimated to between 11 and 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elongated low pressure which eventually intensified offshore along a cold front brought an low end advisory accumulating snowfall to Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter report 3.0 inches at 1 ENE North Haven. A Cooperative Observer reported 2.5 inches at Guilford. The public reported 2.0 inches at 3 ENE Branford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elongated low pressure which eventually intensified offshore along a cold front brought an low end advisory accumulating snowfall to Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The following snow reports were observed by the public; Seymour 4.1 inches and at 1 NW Ansonia 3.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elongated low pressure which eventually intensified offshore along a cold front brought an low end advisory accumulating snowfall to Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported 3.0 inches in Clinton. A trained spotter reported 2.0 inches at 1 SW Westbrook. A Cocorahs observer reported 3.0 inches at 2.9 NNE Old Saybrook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elongated low pressure which eventually intensified offshore along a cold front brought an low end advisory accumulating snowfall to Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observers reported 3.2 inches in Killingworth and 3.0 inches at 1.4 NW Middlefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elongated low pressure which eventually intensified offshore along a cold front brought an low end advisory accumulating snowfall to Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported 5.5 inches at 1 N Lisbon. A cooperative observer reported 2.0 inches in Norwich.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elongated low pressure which eventually intensified offshore along a cold front brought an low end advisory accumulating snowfall to Southern Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observers reported 2.7 inches in Old Lyme, and 2.5 inches in East Lyme. The public reported 4.0 inches in Mystic.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated warm front which resulted in warm advection in the mid levels resulted in an accumulating snow across northwestern portions of Southern Connecticut. The first of 2 accumulating snowfalls within 48 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 5.9 inches at 4 NNW New Fairfield, along with 2.5 inches at 3 WSW Shelton, and 2.5 inches at 3 SSE Weston.  The public reported 3.5 inches at 4 NNW Ridgefield and 2.7 inches at 2 SW Newtown. An emergency manager reported 3.0 inches in Bethel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system emerged out of the Southern Plains and Texas and rode northeast through the Tennessee Valley and the Appalachians, before emerging just off the New Jersey coast. This produced a heavy snowfall across all of Southern Connecticut, with sleet, some of it heavy across a portion of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio reported 15.0 inches in Cheshire, 15.0 inches in Wallingford, and 14.0 inches in Naugatuck. A storm chaser reported 16.0 inches in Meriden. The public reported 12.3 inches at 1 NW Ansonia. Snowfall average amounts were estimated to be above 12 inches.

The public reported 13.0 inches at 1 W North Branford and 12.0 inches in Madison.  Estimated average snowfall amounts for the zone were around 12 inches.

The public reported 2.2 inches in Greenwich, and 2.2 inches at 4 SSE Easton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated warm front which resulted in warm advection in the mid levels resulted in an accumulating snow across northwestern portions of Southern Connecticut. The first of 2 accumulating snowfalls within 48 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported 2.8 inches in Southbury. This was the only report received as the accumulating snow was primarily across northern locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated warm front which resulted in warm advection in the mid levels resulted in an accumulating snow across northwestern portions of Southern Connecticut. The first of 2 accumulating snowfalls within 48 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 1 to 2 inches of snow fell on average across northern portions of the zone. Advisory level snow fell north and west of the zone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system emerged out of the Southern Plains and Texas and rode northeast through the Tennessee Valley and the Appalachians, before emerging just off the New Jersey coast. A trained spotter reported 10.0 inches at 3 ENE Franklin. The average snowfall across the zone was estimated to be around 12.0 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system emerged out of the Southern Plains and Texas and rode northeast through the Tennessee Valley and the Appalachians, before emerging just off the New Jersey coast. This produced a heavy snowfall across all of Southern Connecticut, with sleet, some of it heavy across a portion of the region.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observers reported 4.0 inches at 3.6 SE Salem, 3.7 inches at 2.4 NNW Uncasville, 3.6 inches in Oakdale, and 3.0 inches in Preston. A cooperative observer reported 3.0 inches in Norwich.  Amateur radio reported 3.5 inches in Jewett City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought a widespread moderate accumulation of snow to northwestern Connecticut on January 17, 2026. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 7 inches were common, resulting in slippery travel conditions on area roadways.||Key impacts: travel delays.

A low pressure system brought a widespread moderate accumulation of snow to northwestern Connecticut on January 17, 2026. Snowfall amounts of 3 to 7 inches were common, resulting in slippery travel conditions on area roadways.||Key impacts: travel delays.

A persistent polar airmass created a prolonged period of frigid conditions across northwest Connecticut for the final days of January into the very beginning of February. Bitter cold air temperatures combined with periods of gusty winds and fresh, deep snowpack made for several days worth of dangerously cold conditions. ||The cold outbreak began early on January 29th where minimum wind chill values, or feels-like temperatures, were cold, but not quite to dangerous levels. Feels-like temperatures this first night ranged from about -8 to 2 degrees Fahrenheit. However, by the following night, widespread dangerously cold temperatures overtook the region with minimum air temperatures ranging from -9 to 4 degrees Fahrenheit and minimum feels-like temperatures spanning -20 to -8 degrees Fahrenheit. A brief respite came on January 30th when extensive cloud cover maintained sufficient insulation to limit temperature falls, but by the next night, February 1, minimum feels-like temperatures once again fell to -18 to -7 degrees Fahrenheit.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum low temperature of -3 degrees Fahrenheit recorded with a minimum wind chill of -20 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A blockbuster winter storm that impacted millions across the lower 48, bringing a combination of ice; sleet; snow; rain; and strong winds, brought widespread heavy snowfall accumulations to all of northwest Connecticut from January 25-26th, 2026. ||An arctic blast made for dangerously cold conditions across northwest Connecticut from January 23-24, creating a requisite cold airmass that supported snow as the primary precipitation type with this storm. The connection of the northern and southern stream flow regimes then steered a potent, Gulf-originating low pressure system to the northeast. Strong to anomalous low- to mid-level dynamics drove impressive moisture transport from the Gulf and western Atlantic in and across the region the morning of the impending storm, Sunday January 25th. Snow began Sunday morning, initially with a large dendritic growth zone courtesy of persistent arctic air being filtered into the region from northerly flow about a nearby surface high to the northeast. However, as the storm tracked closer, its associated warm nose modified the thermal profile and shrunk and lifted the dendritic growth zone into the mid and upper levels of the atmosphere by the afternoon. Snowfall rates that initially looked to reach up to 2-4 per hour within laterally translating snow bands therefore became limited to 1-2 per hour courtesy of the disconnect between the best low-level forcing and the dendritic growth zone. Still, a widespread 10-20 of snow fell across the region between Sunday, January 25th and Monday, January 26th. ||The Governor of Connecticut, Ned Lamont, declared a Winter Storm Emergency on January 25th which ultimately led to a travel ban on commercial vehicles on limited-access highways. During this time, residents were also urged to stay off roads. NWS Albany additionally received reports of numerous school and road closures across northwest Connecticut. ||Key Impacts: Winter Storm Emergency declaration, travel restrictions, school closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 14.7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent cold front ushered a bitterly cold airmass into northwest Connecticut on January 23-24, 2026. The combination of bitterly cold air temperatures (minimum temperatures ranging from -6 to 4 degrees Fahrenheit) and gusty winds (ranging from 25 to 35 mph) made for dangerously cold wind chills or feels-like temperatures. Minimum feels-like temperatures ultimately ranged from about -25 to -12 degrees Fahrenheit across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum low temperature of -6 degrees Fahrenheit reported along with a minimum wind chill of -25 degrees Fahrenheit.
